era_context: The early 12th century: Crusader States consolidating in the Levant; the Jin Dynasty (Jurchen) was founded this year and would reshape East Asian politics; the Song Dynasty continued its cultural golden age in southern China; the Seljuk Empire dominated the Near East; Islamic scholarship flourished in Al-Andalus and the broader Islamic world.

events.title: Founding of the Jin Dynasty by Aguda
events.description: Aguda (1068–1123), a Jurchen chieftain, proclaimed himself emperor and founded the Jin Dynasty on 1 January 1115 (or 1115 by the lunar calendar). This marked the beginning of Jurchen political consolidation and the eventual conquest of northern China. The Jin Dynasty would rule until 1234. Aguda's establishment of a centralized state transformed the Jurchen from a tribal confederation into an imperial power.

sources.name: Jin Shi (History of the Jin Dynasty), compiled in the 13th century
Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty), references to Jurchen
Yuan Haowen and other contemporary observers

events.certainty_note: The founding of the Jin Dynasty in 1115 is well documented in Chinese historical sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Crusader military campaigns in the Levant under Baldwin II
events.region: Kingdom of Jerusalem and surrounding territories
events.description: King Baldwin II of Jerusalem (r. 1100–1118) continued military campaigns to expand and consolidate Crusader territorial control. In 1115, Baldwin was engaged in campaigns against Muslim forces and in establishing Crusader fortifications. These campaigns were part of the broader consolidation of the Kingdom of Jerusalem following the First Crusade.
key_figures: Joscelin I of Courtenay (Count of Edessa)
events.certainty_note: Crusader military activity in this period is documented, though specific campaigns in 1115 are not always precisely dated in sources.

events.title: Seljuk Empire under Muhammad I Tapar; administrative and cultural development
events.description: Muhammad I Tapar (r. 1105–1118) ruled the Great Seljuk Empire during a period of administrative consolidation and cultural flourishing. Under Tapar, the Seljuk state developed sophisticated bureaucratic structures influenced by Persian administrative traditions. The empire was at the height of its power and cultural achievement.
key_figures: Nizam al-Mulk (administrative tradition, though he died in 1092)
events.certainty_note: Seljuk history in this period is well documented in Persian and Islamic sources.

events.title: Song Dynasty technological and artistic achievements
events.description: The Northern Song Dynasty continued its period of extraordinary technological and artistic achievement. In 1115, advances in ceramics (particularly celadon and other high-fired wares), printing technology, mathematical treatises, and astronomical observation were ongoing. The imperial court under Emperor Huizong (r. 1100–1126) was a major patron of the arts.
key_figures: Su Shi (polymath, though he died in 1101)

sources.name: Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Song Dynasty cultural achievements are well documented through textual and archaeological evidence.

events.title: Islamic scholarship and mathematics in Al-Andalus
events.description: In the early 12th century, Al-Andalus was a center of Islamic scholarship, mathematics, astronomy, and philosophy. By 1115, scholars in cities like Cordoba, Toledo, and Granada were producing significant works in mathematics, medicine, and natural philosophy. This period saw the transmission of Islamic knowledge to Christian Europe through translation and cultural contact.
events.certainty_note: The flourishing of Islamic scholarship in Al-Andalus during this period is well documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No significant disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed. The founding date of the Jin Dynasty (1115) is secure in Chinese historical sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1115 CE is moderate to good. The founding of the Jin Dynasty is a major historical event with secure dating and rich documentation in Chinese sources. Crusader history is well documented through contemporary chronicles. Song Dynasty history is richly documented. Islamic history in this period is moderately documented. Ethiopian, sub-Saharan African, and pre-Columbian American history for this year are essentially undocumented in surviving written sources.

graph_edges.note: Jin Dynasty founding in 1115 led to conquest of Northern Song in 1127
Jin Dynasty founded in 1115; fell in 1234
Crusader military campaigns continued from 1115 into 1116
